text: The surgeon begins the robotic sleeve gastrectomy with a 36 French bougie in place. Using a bipolar vessel sealer, they access the lesser sac by creating a window at the inferior portion of the stomach and proceed with dissection distally, maintaining a distance of approximately six centimeters from the pylorus. The insessura is identified as a key landmark, noting the sharp turn of the stomach to avoid narrowing.

The surgeon uses a bipolar vessel sealer to dissect the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, mobilizing this section before creating the sleeve. The left diaphragm is exposed, and posterior attachments are released, ensuring the stomach is fully mobilized for optimal sleeve formation.

The surgeon positions the bougie and begins stapling the stomach to form the sleeve, starting from the marked spot. A blue staple load is used, and the stapler is clamped to allow tissue edema to reduce before firing. The stapling progresses with compression, using five staple loads to complete the sleeve gastrectomy, ensuring no excess pouching and a well-formed sleeve near the diaphragm.

The surgical field shows the hiatus with minimal excess tissue, indicating a well-formed sleeve gastrectomy. The surgeon adjusts the table to a 15-degree angle for a leak test and removes the bougie. A small area at the top of the sleeve requires hemostasis.

The surgeon applies fibrin sealant to the completed sleeve gastrectomy, which was performed using a thirty-six French bougie without an assist port at a pressure of ten. The sleeve appears well-formed, and the trocar is removed, followed by dilation of the hole with a Kelly clamp, maintaining visualization with the air seal system.
